tossups.question: In an Atwood story named for this poem, Bill breaks up with the narrator because she empathizes with this poem’s villain. Another response to this poem describes “How walls / rise out of water yet appear to recede / identically / into it” and recommends paying a dowry off slowly. That poem is Richard Howard’s zigzag-formatted “Nikolaus Mardruz to his Master Ferdinand.” This poem describes a figure with “depth and passion of its earnest glance” and a “faint half-flush that dies along her throat.” The narrator of this poem refuses to say “Just this or that in you disgusts me,” claiming “I never (*) stoop.” This poem’s subject, who smiles at a white mule or a bough of cherries, has “a heart...too soon made glad” and is painted by Fra Pandolf. For 10 points, name this Robert Browning monologue in which the Duke reminisces about his wife he had killed.
tossups.question_sanitized: In an Atwood story named for this poem, Bill breaks up with the narrator because she empathizes with this poem's villain. Another response to this poem describes "How walls / rise out of water yet appear to recede / identically / into it" and recommends paying a dowry off slowly. That poem is Richard Howard's zigzag-formatted "Nikolaus Mardruz to his Master Ferdinand." This poem describes a figure with "depth and passion of its earnest glance" and a "faint half-flush that dies along her throat." The narrator of this poem refuses to say "Just this or that in you disgusts me," claiming "I never (*) stoop." This poem's subject, who smiles at a white mule or a bough of cherries, has "a heart...too soon made glad" and is painted by Fra Pandolf. For 10 points, name this Robert Browning monologue in which the Duke reminisces about his wife he had killed.

bonuses.leadin: The fate of this character is intertwined with that of Isabel Archer in the second movement of Kate Soper’s Voices from the Killing Jar. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: The fate of this character is intertwined with that of Isabel Archer in the second movement of Kate Soper's Voices from the Killing Jar. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this character, who is accused of equating a man’s “favour at her breast” with “the dropping of the daylight in the West”. After this character’s disappearance, it is claimed that “all smiles stopped together”.
In a story by this author, Nell fights with Bill, who labels the duchess of Ferrara a “simp” after they read Robert Browning’s “My Last Duchess”. This Canadian author wrote The Handmaid’s Tale.

“My Last Duchess” is the basis for this contemporary author’s novel The Marriage Portrait. In a novel by this author, the son of the ‘Latin tutor’ passes away after “decreeing” that his sister Judith must live.
parts_sanitized: Name this character, who is accused of equating a man's "favour at her breast" with "the dropping of the daylight in the West". After this character's disappearance, it is claimed that "all smiles stopped together".
In a story by this author, Nell fights with Bill, who labels the duchess of Ferrara a "simp" after they read Robert Browning's "My Last Duchess". This Canadian author wrote The Handmaid's Tale.

"My Last Duchess" is the basis for this contemporary author's novel The Marriage Portrait. In a novel by this author, the son of the 'Latin tutor' passes away after "decreeing" that his sister Judith must live.
answers: “my last duchess” [or Lucrezia de’ Medici, Duchess of Ferrara, prompt on Medici]
answers_sanitized: "my last duchess" [or Lucrezia de' Medici, Duchess of Ferrara, prompt on Medici]

bonuses.leadin: This poet asked “What of the leafage, what of the flower?” and wrote “Come then, complete incompletion” in his poem “Wanting is — what?”, the introduction to his collection Jocoseria. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this poet whose “Home-Thoughts, from Abroad” opens with the lines “Oh, to be in England / Now that April's there”.

The most famous of Browning’s dramatic monologues may be this one, whose speaker examines a portrait by Fra Pandolf of the title woman, who had a heart “too soon made glad”, presumably causing the speaker to have her killed.
The speaker says that he will “summon age / to grant youth's heritage” in this Browning poem, a dramatic monologue that begins “Grow old along with me! The best is yet to be.”
